titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Pugin family archive
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Emily Crow, Meg Rinn, YCBA Staff

publicationstmt.publisher: Yale Center for British Art, Archives

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Yale Center for British Art, Archives

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday February 25, 2026 at 18:00

did.unittitle: Pugin family archive

corpname.part: Yale Center for British Art, Archives

did.abstract: The collection comprises correspondence and other manuscript material of the English architect Augustus W.N. Pugin, his parents Augustus C. Pugin and Catherine Welby Pugin, Augustus W. N.'s son Edward, Catherine's siblings Selina and Adlard, and Augustus W. N.'s associate Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps de Lisle.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The collection is comprised of correspondence and other manuscript material concerning the family of Augustus Welby Northmore Pugin (1812-1852). About 50 items in the collection directly concern A.W.N. Pugin, including his correspondence, lecture notes, and accounts. Principal correspondents include William Leigh, John Fuller Russell, and Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps De Lisle.
A large portion of the collection is comprised of correspondence of Augustus W.N.'s mother Catherine Welby Pugin (1768-1833), primarily with her siblings, Selina Welby and Adlard Welby.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The collection is the physical property of the Yale Center for British Art. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the Archives Department.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Pugin Family Archive, Yale Center for British Art, Paul Mellon Collection

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Yale Center for British Art, Paul Mellon Collection

odd.head: General
odd.p: Material was previously microfilmed, with each individual item assigned a call number following the format "Pugin-#." The original number is recorded in the general notes field of each item for reference.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection is comprised of correspondence and other manuscript material concerning the family of Augustus Welby Northmore Pugin (1812-1852), including his father Augustus Charles Pugin, his mother Catherine Welby Pugin, and Catherine's siblings Adlard Welby and Selina Welby. Also present is manuscript material relating to Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps De Lisle, a prominent English Catholic who Augustus W. N. Pugin was in frequent contact with.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Material is arranged into nine series: Series I. Augustus Charles Pugin; Series II. Augustus W.N. Pugin; Series III. Catherine Welby Pugin; Series IV. Edward Welby Pugin; Series V. Welby family; Series VI. Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps de Lisle; Series VII. Miscellaneous correspondence and notes; Series VIII. Printed material; Series IX. Microfilm print outs.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Augustus Charles Pugin was born in Paris, France in 1768 or 1769 to Francois Joseph Pugin and his second wife Marguerite Duchene. He was one of seven surviving children and his father self-identified as a fils de laboureur (son of a laborer), despite later self-propogated stories that Pugin was descended from nobility. He most likely grew up among painters and craftsmen, before leaving for London in the early 1790s, for unknown reasons. He may have left to pursue a career as an artist or escape the French Revolution.

After his arrival in London, Pugin enrolled in the Royal Academy schools in March 1792. He also began working as a draftsman for the architect Josh Nash sometime around 1790 to 1792. Nash was at a precarious moment in his own career at the time - his designs for the Royal Pavilion, Brighton, Buckingham Palace, and Regents Street would come later. While Pugin would create a variety of drawings for Nash for the rest of his career, he was not involved in his most famous projects. Taking inspiration from the aesthetic theory of the Picturesque, Augustus C. Pugin illustrated buildings for Nash, both of his existing and proposed projects. Because of this work, Pugin became a member of the first generation of architectural perspectivists.

Pugin and Nash's relationship broke down around 1801, and Pugin had to increasingly seek freelance work in London. Despite his relationship with Nash, Pugin could not find work as an independent architect. Instead, he worked within the worlds of art, design, and architecture while illustrating buildings, fashion, and significant events. These inconsistent commissions meant he was struggling to make a living when he met and married his wife Catherine Welby in 1802. However, it was his commission to illustrate scenes from Lord Nelson's funeral that gained the attention of Rudolph Ackermann and would result in the most well-known project of his career.

He met Rudolph Ackerman, a German-born publisher and print seller in 1806. Ackerman's patronage would lead to his work on The Microcosm of London , a three-volume collection of images and descriptions of London. The plates for The Microcosm of London are a composition of A.C. Pugin's architectural drawings of sites in London and Thomas Rowlandson's figures populating the buildings. Published between 1808 and 1810, the Microcosm was aimed at the wealthy and avoided depicting the more plebian institutions of London. However, it still celebrated the city's diverse populations and activities. Ackermann's intent was to provide a type of visual travel guide, which now preserves his and his artists' vision of early nineteenth century London.

Catherine gave birth to their son Augustus W. N. Pugin in 1812. Augustus C. Pugin's career took another positive turn in 1814 when he developed a fruitful personal and professional relationship with French painter and illustrator Louis Lafitte. Lafitte had married Pugin's sister Jeanne Adélaїde in 1795, and with England and France now at peace, the two childhood friends could cross the English Channel and help each other develop relationships with patrons and acquire important commissions. Additionally, in 1819, Pugin began taking on students and, with the assistance of Catherine, ran a drawing school for young men.

Though his career suffered after the death of Lafitte in 1828, Pugin never stopped working as an illustrator. Despite these commissions and the loyalty of Ackermann who often loaned Pugin money , he and Catherine were never financially secure. He died in December 1832, survived by Catherine and his son Augustus W.N. Pugin.

Sources: Hill, Rosemary. "A. C. Pugin." The Burlington Magazine 138, no. 1114 (Jan., 1996): 11-19. Hill, Rosemary. God's architect: Pugin and the building of romantic Britain. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2009

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Augustus Welby Northmore Pugin (1812 March 1 - 1852 September 14) was born to Augustus C. Pugin and Catherine Welby Pugin in Bloomsbury, London, England. He is best known as a pioneer of Gothic Revival architecture and for his designs of the palace of Westminster, including the clock tower that houses the bell known as Big Ben.

Pugin's father, Augustus Charles, was an artist himself and the family ran a drawing school out of their home. His mother, Catherine, was heavily involved in his upbringing. Augustus W.N. would go on to work in his father's studio for a brief time before turning to furniture design work, including work at Windsor Castle through Morel and Seddon. At age 19, he was creating theatrical scenery at King's Theatre, London. He began to seriously pursue architecture as a career in the early 1830s, which included a tour of the European continent as a form of self-instruction. He began working for James Gillepsie Graham around 1834.

That same year saw Augustus convert to Catholicism. Around this time, the status of Catholicism was undergoing a shift in England, and discrimination against Catholics began to lessen. Pugin's conversion built a particular social circle for Pugin of other fellow English Catholics. This included John Talbot, 16th Earl of Shewsbury, and Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps de Lisle.

Pugin's major works include the Palace of Westminster with its bell tower home to Big Ben, St. Mary's Derby, St. Chard's in Birmingham, additions to Alton Towers and Alton Castle, and Mount St. Bernard's Abbey, among others.

Pugin's interest and advocacy for Gothic architecture, which would contribute to the Gothic Revival, did not come from one precise moment. Working with his father, Augustus published Specimens of Gothic Architecture and Examples of Gothic Architecture. Likewise, his work for firms like Mordel and Seddon meant he was already familiar with the style and well versed in it, as well as his experiences and exposure to medieval architecture and Catholicism. These and other factors resulted in Pugin linking a moral society to the kind of architecture produced. For him, Gothic architecture created a principled Christian society, one that was worth trying to revive. This was articulated in his written works on the subject including Contrasts (1836) and The True Principles of Pointed or Christian Architecture (1844).

Augustus W. N. Pugin married three times. His first wife was Anne Garnet, who he wed in 1831. She died in May 1832 following complications giving birth to their daughter, Anne. Louisa Button married Pugin in June 1833. Together they had six children, Edward Welby Pugin (1834-1875); Agnes Pugins (1836-1872); Cuthbert Pugin (1840-1928); Katherine Pugin (1841-1927); and Mary Pugin (1843-1933). Following Louisa's death, Pugin married Jane Knill in 1848, resulting in two children, Margaret Pugin (1849-1884) and Peter Paul Pugin (181-1904).

In 1852, Augustus W. N. Pugin fell ill. The exact nature of the illness is unclear, but symptoms manifested as primarily psychological based on contemporary accounts. At times, he would forget his own name or fall into manic states. Due to the nature of these symptoms, he was placed in private asylums. Eventually, he was given permission to leave and was released into the care of his family, primarily his third wife, Jane. He died at home in Ramsgate on September 14, 1852.
Sources: Hill, Rosemary. God's architect: Pugin and the building of romantic Britain. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2009

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Catherine Northmore Welby Pugin

Catherine Northmore Welby Pugin (1768 or 1769-1833) was born in Denton, Lincoln, England to William Earle Welby and his second wife, Elizabeth Caesar. She had three siblings, with two surviving into adulthood: Selina and Adlard. Their branch of the Welby family was considered the "poor relations" by other, wealthier branches, but it did mean that the Welbys had access to high society. Catherine was noted as having a strong personality, and she was interested in topics such as women's rights and the better treatment of animals.

It is unclear where Catherine and Augustus Charles Pugin met. The two were married in 1802 with Catherine's sister Selina in attendance. Catherine's partnership with her husband was strong, as correspondence shows him asking her for advice, and she was actively involved in managing his career and later the drawing school that they ran out of their home. Catherine had one child, Augustus Welby Northmore Pugin, who was born on March 1, 1812. She was an invested mother and highly involved with his care and upbringing, going so far as to keep notes about his growth and development.

As her son grew older, Catherine was involved in his education which included reading, writing, and drawing. She also continued to manage the drawing school and remained supportive of both her son's endeavors and her husband's career. Catherine remained in close contact with both of her siblings as well.

In her later years, Catherine continued to be actively involved with her son's life. This included travel and moral support following the death of Augustus W. N.'s first wife Anne Garnet in May 1832, due to complications in childbirth. She not only helped to look after her grandchild – also named Anne – but her husband Augustus C. Pugin whose health was failing. He died in December 1832.
Catherine herself died of an unknown cause in April 1833. She is buried in St. Mary's Churchyard, Westminster, London.
Sources: Hill, Rosemary. God's architect: Pugin and the building of romantic Britain. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2009

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Adlard Welby (1776 October 3-1861, Feb 12) was the youngest child of William and Elizabeth Welby. He had three siblings including Catherine, who would later marry August Charles Pugin, and Selina Welby. All three siblings kept in close touch with each other throughout the years.
The Lincolnshire Archives holds the journals of Adlard Welby (call number MISC DON 969) which start in 1843 and continue until 1855 which gives insight into his the later years of his life.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Selina Welby (1759 or 1770-1834 September 4) was the daughter of William Earle Welby and Elizabeth Caesar. Little is known of her life, although she was in close contact with her sister Catherine and brother Adlard. She was heavily involved with the upbringing of her nephew, August W. N, Pugin. She died September 4, 1834.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps de Lisle (17 March 1809 – 5 March 1878) was a wealthy man in British society and best known for advocating for the reconciliation of Britian with Catholicism. The son of Charles March-Phillipps and Harriet Ducarel, de Lisle was raised in the Church of England. While at private school, he encountered Catholicism for the first time in the form of a Catholic priest. He maintained an abiding interest in the faith which was reinforced through a trip to Paris in 1823. He converted soon after.
De Lisle attended Trinity College, University of Cambridge, beginning in 1826. He went on to marry Laura Mary Clifford on July 25, 1833. Together the had 16 children.

Thanks to his wealth, Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps de Lisle was able to fund numerous building projects connected to his desire to reconcile Britain with the Catholic faith. He worked with fellow Catholic and noted architect Augustus W. N. Pugin to build Mount St. Bernard Abbey near Coalville, Leicestershire, England, the first monastery built in Britain since the Protestant Reformation. The project began in 1835 and was completed in 1844 with support from another fellow Catholic, John Talbot, 16th Earl of Shrewsbury. Pugin would work with de Lisle on numerous other projects, including de Lisle's home of Grace-Dieu Manor, Turry Log School, and Shepshed Roman Catholic Church.

Beyond building projects, Ambrose worked to advance his Roman Catholic faith by creating organizations and encouraging missions. Most notably, he founded the Association for Promoting the Unity of Christendom, which united different branches of Christianity in prayer. However, the Vatican condemned the organization, causing it to close.
Ambrose maintained an active correspondence with fellow British Catholics throughout his life. He died on March 5, 1878 at age 69 in his home of Grace-Dieu Manor.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Edward Welby Pugin was born to Augustus W. N. Pugin and his second wife Louisa Button on 11 March, 1834. His mother died in 1844, and Edward was raised by his father and stepmother Jane Knill in Ramsgate. Known at home as Teddy, Edward helped in his father's office from the age of seven. Once his father grew ill in 1852, Edward took over the practice. Upon Augustus W.N. Pugin's death, Edward and his stepmother moved households and took the firm to Birmingham, partly to be near their main patron Bertram Talbot, the 17th Earl of Shrewsbury.

Edward's early career included completing some of his father's commissions, though he soon developed his own style that more closely resembled the flamboyant Franco-Flemish Gothic. At age 24, he was designated a Knight of the Order of St Sylvester by Pope Pius IX after his design for the Basilica of the Immaculate Conception in Dadizele, Belgium. In 1862, Edward Pugin was elected a Fellow of the Royal Institute of British Architects. He continued to design churches and cathedrals throughout Britain, Ireland, and Belgium, and aided in the design of the stained glass and furnishings contained within. Edward's other projects included designs for monasteries, schools/colleges, orphanages, and convalescent homes.
Edward differed from his father in that he regularly participated in the society of Ramsgate. He became known as a dandy and for being somewhat adversarial to his peers, writing pamphlets attacking other architects. He also made several bad investments after his architectural output peaked in the mid 1860s, resulting in the liquidation of his estate. He sought opportunities in the United States of America in 1873, but never completed a project and left less than a year later.

By 1874, Edward had been expelled from the RIBA and was bogged down by court cases over libel and the stress of what was still a heavy workload. Days before his death, he visited the construction site of his design for OMI Juniorate College for the Oblates of Mary Immaculate. He died in the arms of his brother Cuthbert at age 41 from syncope of the heart, possibly the result of his regular use of chloral hydrate. Though his career only lasted 23 years, Edward designed over 100 churches and cathedrals, with his brother Peter Paul Pugin taking over the firm Pugin and Pugin after his death.

Sources: Hill, Rosemary. God's architect: Pugin and the building of romantic Britain. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2009 Hyland, GJ. "Biographical Sketch of Edward Welby Pugin, 1834-75." The Pugin Society, 2010. http://www.thepuginsociety.co.uk/bio.html

did.unittitle: Series I. Augustus Charles Pugin

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Correspondence has been placed first and arranged chronologically, followed by additional writings which have been arranged alphabetically by title.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence in this series often reflects unknown senders writing to Augustus Charles Pugin. Also included are several commentaries and prospectuses, primarily for Pugin's Views of London and Westminster .

did.unittitle: Series II. Augustus W.N. Pugin

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Correspondence has been placed first and arranged chronologically, followed by additional writings which have been arranged alphabetically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Material reflects Augustus W. N. Pugin's work as an architect and the social contacts he made in the course of his work. Topics in the correspondence include building activities in Woodchester Park and Castle Chapel at Alton Towers, in addition to other projects and concerns. Other material includes an account sheet, lecture notes regarding architecture, and additional notes.

did.unittitle: Unknown sender in Hunts Green MS letter to A.W.N. Pugin

persname.part: Beresford Hope, A. J. B. (Alexander James Beresford), 1820-1887

Pugin, Professor of Ecclesiastical Antiquities in that College/Lecture the first". Later published as part of The true principles of pointed or Christian architecture .

scopecontent.p: Notes on French Gothic churches, museums, curiosities.

did.unittitle: Series III. Catherine Welby Pugin

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Correspondence has been placed first and arranged chronologically, followed by additional writings which have been arranged alphabetically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence in this collection reflects Catherine's life prior to her marriage to Augustus Charles Pugin and after. It includes messages with her siblings, especially Selina, associates such as the Chomeleys and Charlotte Want, and some writing between her and her husband Augustus Charles Pugin. Also present are several notes that reflect her thoughts on marraige, architectural history, and other topics.

did.unittitle: Unknown sender in Grantham letter to Catherine Welby Pugin, at Allington

did.unittitle: Catherine Welby Pugin notes regarding "Mr. C"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: By Cathering Welby Pugin? Concerning A. C. Pugin and his work to be done at Easton for Mr. C. [Sir Montague Cholmeley?] Written on verso of letter cover addressed to Mrs. Pugin at Islington; postmarked JY 8, 1803.

did.unittitle: Catherine Welby Pugin notes regarding architectural history

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: By Catherine Welby Pugin? Text frp, unidentified historical work, "First Edition 1598." Written in margins and on verso of letter cover addressed to Mrs. Pugin at Islington; postmarked 9 AU, 1803[?]

did.unittitle: Series IV. Edward Welby Pugin

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Correspondence has been placed first and arranged chronologically, followed by additional writings which have been arranged alphabetically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Material primarily consists of two diaries written by Edwin covering the year 1854.

did.unittitle: J. Lutton letter to Edward Welby Pugin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Boulers.

did.unittitle: Edward Welby Pugin Diary

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daily work record for Edward Welby Pugin, Edmund Kirby, and J.W. [or L.W. or T.W.?]

did.unittitle: Edward Welby Pugin notes on Augstus W.N. Pugin's designs for Charles Barry and James Gillspie Graham; unknown sender letter to the "Editor of the Nation" on verso

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: By E.W. Pugin? On verso, letter "To the Editor of the Nation" in another hand.

did.unittitle: Series V. Welby family

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Material has been arranged alphabetically by the first name of the sender, then chronologically.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The bulk of material in this series consists of correspondence from Adlard Welby and from Selina Welby to their sibling, Catherine Welby Pugin. This correspondence tracks daily life and concerns among the siblings. Also present is a small volume of additional correspondence from Adlard to others. A single letter from Richard Earle Welby is also included.

did.unittitle: Adlard Welby letter to Catherine Welby Pugin, at Islington
Adlard Welby letter to Catherine Welby Pugin, at Middle Temple

Series VI. Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps de Lisle

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Material in the series reflects the interest of Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps de Lisle regarding Catholicism in England and his Association for the Promotion of the Unity of Christendom.

did.unittitle: Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps de Lisle letter to John Talbot, 16th Earl of Shrewsbury

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Garendon Park.

did.unittitle: Frederick George Lee letter to Ambrose Lisle March Phillipps de Lisle
Series VII. Miscellaneous correspondence and notes

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Material consists of correspondence and notes in unidentified hands. The correspondence is arranged alphabetically by last name of sender and then chronologically. Notes have been placed following the correspondence and arranged alphabetically.

did.unittitle: J. C. Collins letter [to Laura May De Lisle?]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Hare Court.

did.unittitle: J. C. Collins note [to Laura May De Lisle?]
Richard Doyle letter extract to Lady Duff Gordon

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Concerns the deaths of A.W.N. Pugin and the Duke of Wellington.

persname.part: Duff Gordon, Joanna Maria Grant, d. 1872

did.unittitle: Susan Kean letter to L.T. Peniston, at Ramsgate

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Tolnay Street.

did.unittitle: Elizabeth Cox Knill letter [to Jane Knill Pugin?]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Elliot Vale.

did.unittitle: Elizabeth Cox Knill letter [to Jane Knill Pugin?]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "My dearest Jane."

did.unittitle: Elizabeth Cox Knill letter [to Jane Knill Pugin?]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Addington Placida.

did.unittitle: Charles Francis Hansom letter to William Leigh, at Woodchester Park

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Coventry. Notes in another hand on verso. Concerns improvements to be made in the church at Woodchester Park. In pencil in another hand, annotated H1. Housed with Pugin 222-233.

did.unittitle: Charles Francis Hansom letter to William Leigh

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Coventry. Concerns estimates for building at Woodchester Park. In pnecil in another hand, annotated H2. Housed with Pugin 222-233.

did.unittitle: Charles Francis Hansom letter to William Leigh, at Lemmington

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Coventry. Notes in another hand on verso. Concerns building activities at Woodchester Park. In pencil in another hand, annotated H3. Housed with Pugin 222-233.

did.unittitle: Charles Francis Hansom letter [to William Leigh]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Clifton. Concerns building activities at Woodchester Park. In pencil in another hand, annotated H4. Housed with Pugin 222-233.

did.unittitle: Frederick Oakeley letter to unknown recipient

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Oxford. Addressed to "My dear Sir." Inscribed in another hand: "Red[?] to Oakeley October 18."

did.unittitle: John Bernard Palmer letter to John Talbot, 16th Earl of Shrewsbury

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inscribed "N.B. To be immediately considered & returned to me."

did.unittitle: John Bernard Palmer letter to John Talbot, 16th Earl of Shrewsbury

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Mount St. Bernard. Inscribed "Pugin. To be returned."

did.unittitle: Envelope addressed to Mrs. Jane Pugin, at Ramsgate

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Postmarked AU 17, 1849.

did.unittitle: Sophie [?] letter to unknown recipient

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Signed "Sophie."

did.unittitle: R. Storr letter to Sir Montague Cholmeley

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Grantham. Enclosed in letter from Sir Montague Cholmeley to Catherine Welby Pugin (MS Pugin 115)?

did.unittitle: Wyse, Thomas at Wilton Place letter to unknown recipient

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Typed letter (copy?). Written from Wilton Place. Concerns work of A.W.N. Pugin, Lord Midleton.

did.unittitle: Winifriede M. Wyse letter to Elizabeth Knill Pugin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Hove.

did.unittitle: Letter to the editor regarding a cathedral planned for London with notes

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed "To the Editor of the Nation." Concerns new cathedral planned for London. Notes [by Edward Welby Pugin?] on verso.

did.unittitle: Unknown sender (draft) letter to "My Lord"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "My Lord." Concerns London animal markets.

did.unittitle: Unknown sender (draft) letter to "My Lord"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "My Lord." Concerns London animal markets.

did.unittitle: Unknown sender (draft) letter [to Sir Montague Cholmeley?]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Fragment of letter from Berkeley Square on verso.

did.unittitle: Unknown sender (draft) letter to unknown recipient

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Sir." Written in margins and on verso of prospectus for Select views of Bath, Bristol, Malvern, Cheltenham, and Weymouth; drawings by John Claude Nattes, engraved by A. Pugin .

did.unittitle: Unknown sender (fragment) letter to unknown recipient
Unknown sender at Islington letter to unknown recipient

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Written from Islington. Addressed to "Sir."

did.unittitle: Unknown sender letter to unknown recipient

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Addressed to "Sir" and signed "A constant reader." Concerns Mr. Windham's speech in House of Commons on the punishment of cruelty to animals.

The Crosier of William of Wykeham

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Inscribed "Extracted from the letter of Revd. Dr. Oglander, Warden of New College Oxford, which was addressed to Mr. Carter, and inserted in his work on 'Specimens of Ancient Sculpture and Painting' published in 1795-"

Samuel Birch Rules of the Quintette Musical Society

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Signed "S. Birch, Secretary." The society was instituted 13 November 1793.

did.unittitle: Westminster Hall
Series VIII. Printed material

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Material in this series represents unbound bookpages, pamplets, and other printed items including the handwritten correspondence and notes in the collection.
